title: Kth Smallest and Largest in BST

prerequisites: MAXIMUM NODE VALUE IN BINARY TREE

statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary search tree and an integer k, return the kth smallest and kth largest element in the BST. The problem requires finding both the kth smallest and kth largest elements. The BST is a binary tree where for every node, the values in the left child are less than the node value and the values in the right child are greater than the node value. You should assume that 1 <= k <= n, where n is the number of nodes in the BST.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of nodes in the BST. The next lines contain the node values, one per line, representing an in-order traversal of the BST. The last line contains the value of k.
statement.outputFormat: Two integers, one per line, representing the kth smallest and kth largest elements in the BST.

constraints.description: The position of the element to find
The number of nodes in the BST

examples.explanation: The in-order traversal of the BST is 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. The 2nd smallest element is 2 and the 2nd largest element is 4.
The in-order traversal of the BST is 1, 2, 3. The 2nd smallest element is 2 and the 2nd largest element is 2.

hints.text: The problem involves a binary search tree (BST) and requires finding the kth smallest and kth largest elements. Consider how the properties of a BST can be leveraged to find these elements efficiently.

In a BST, for any given node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater than the node. This property can be used to traverse the tree in a way that helps find the kth smallest and kth largest elements.

One approach to solving this problem is to perform a traversal of the BST that allows you to visit nodes in an ordered manner. Consider using an in-order traversal or a similar strategy to find the kth smallest element. For the kth largest, think about how you can adapt the traversal to visit nodes in descending order.

editorial.title: Finding Kth Smallest and Largest in BST
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by traversing the BST in a way that allows us to find the kth smallest and largest elements efficiently. A brute force approach would involve traversing the entire tree and storing the elements in a list, then sorting the list to find the kth smallest and largest elements.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves traversing the BST, storing its elements in a list, sorting the list, and then finding the kth smallest and largest elements. This approach is correct but slow, with a time complexity of O(n log n) due to the sorting step, and a space complexity of O(n) for storing the elements.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach is to utilize the properties of a BST and perform an in-order traversal to find the kth smallest element, and a reverse in-order traversal to find the kth largest element.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves performing two separate traversals of the BST: an in-order traversal to find the kth smallest element, and a reverse in-order traversal to find the kth largest element. This approach has a time complexity of O(h + k) for each traversal, where h is the height of the tree, and a space complexity of O(h) for the recursion stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that an in-order traversal of a BST visits the nodes in ascending order, and a reverse in-order traversal visits the nodes in descending order. By stopping the traversal when the kth node is reached, we can find the kth smallest and largest elements efficiently.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with a sample BST and values of k and n confirms that it produces the correct results.
commonMistakes: Not utilizing the properties of a BST to optimize the solution
Using a brute force approach with a time complexity of O(n log n)
Not considering the space complexity of the solution
Not handling the case where k is equal to 1 or n
Not verifying the correctness of the solution with test cases
Not considering the trade-offs between time and space complexity

functionSignature.java: public int[] kthSmallestLargest(TreeNode root, int k);
functionSignature.python: def kthSmallestLargest(self, root: TreeNode, k: int) -> list[int]:
